narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and undergraduate GPA. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 61.6% chance that you might not, which is above the threshold we set for approving applications—leading to the rejection of yours. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile in your third year of school, where you ranked in the bottom tenth compared to your peers, making it the single biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your first-year decile, also on the lower end, added to that concern, though with less weight. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, which is close to the typical student's, strongly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, suggesting you'd likely pass if that held up. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, just a bit below average, helped counterbalance some risks by nudging toward success. Being a full-time student aligned with the norm for most applicants and provided a small positive influence, while your middle-income family background had minimal impact either way. Compared to typical students who get approved, your third-year performance stood out as weaker than the average mid-range decile, tipping the scales despite your other solid metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many factors like these are just snapshots of past performance. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year grades, as that's the key area the model flagged, and perhaps seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You have the drive to overcome this; reapplying with strengthened academics could change the outcome next time. We're rooting for you.
